content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistence • • • Administration • • • Executive-Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660            322-5901         322-6441            322-6444

March 13, 1980

Gary Shapiro
2720 Central Avenue
McKinleyville, CA 95521

Dear Mr. Shapiro:

Thank you for your letter dated February 29, 1980,
seeking advice regarding the disqualification provisions of
the Political Reform Act pursuant to Government Code Section
83114(b). The facts, as I understand them, may be stated as
follows.

You present serve on the CETA Private Industry Council
for Humboldt County. The Council is charged with the respon-
sibility for evaluating programs that would promote the
growth of employment and small businesses in Humboldt County.
One of the Council's duties is to recommend to the Board of
Supervisors the extent to which the County should fund such
programs. One program evaluated by the Council and later
approved for funding by the Board of Supervisors was developed
by a nonprofit organization called the Redwood Community
Development Council (RCDC). RCDC's program calls for the
establishment of a support center to provide small business
owners with new management skills and marketing techniques.
Any small business in the county may participate in the
program; there will be no screening by either RCDC or the
CETA Council. The question that you have asked our office
to consider is whether it would be a conflict of interest
for your business to participate in the program.

Government Code Section 37100 provides:

    No public official at any level of state or local
    government shall make, participate in making or in
    any way attempt to use his official position to
    influence a governmental decision in which he
    knows or has reason to know he has a financial
    interest.

Gary Shapiro
March 13, 1980
Page Two


Government Code Section 87103 provides:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
     within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is rea-
     sonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
     material financial effect, distinguishable from
     its effect on the public generally, on:

          (a)  Any business entity in which the public
               official has a direct or indirect investment
               worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

The Act, therefore, prohibits you as a public official
from participating in governmental decision, including those
of the CETA Council that could have a foreseeable material
effect on your business.  The Act does not, however, regulate
your private business decisions, including whether or not to
participate in the program developed by RCDC.

     I hope that this advice is helpful.  If our office can
be of further assistance, please do not hesitate to contact
us.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Dwight E. Dickerson
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

DED:kp

Overland
Stoneware

2720 Central Avenue
McKinleyville, Ca 95521
707 834-3111

February 29, 1980

Mr. Dwight Dickerson
Coordinator
P.O. Box 307
San Lorenzo, CA 94580

Dear Mr. Dickerson,

Enclosed is V. Jacobella's O. on the I.I.D.A. CD the S.B.A. in
regards to a small business loan and other operational information
which I feel would be of interest to you in connection with my
request for a loan to cover the cost of manufacturing equipment
of Overland Stoneware. As business would like to be involved
with S.B.A. programs, I have been advised by the R.T.D.B. that
staff in the participation of Overland Stoneware in the S.B.A.
program would in no way exclude any other business from partici-
pating in the program. The question, then, is whether or not
this constitute a conflict of interest on my part. Your opinion
would be appreciated in this matter.

If you have any further questions, please feel free to call.

Thank you for the help.

Sincerely,

Gary Shoppers
V. Shoppe

Mr. Donald Pollard
January 21, 1980

Page Two

Government Code Section 87100 provides that,

No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in
making or in any way attempt to use his position
to influence a governmental decision in which
he knows or has reason to know he has a finan-
cial interest.
[Emphasis added.]

This provision of the Political Reform Act prohibits
public officials from participating in governmental decisions
in which they are financially interested. When you cease to
be a public official within the meaning of the Act, therefore,
the disqualification provision can no longer apply to you. Thus,
after you have left government service you will not be pro-
hibited by the Act from doing private consultant work in the
city of Pasadena, including representing clients before the
city.

The Act does not prohibit the city from retaining you to
do part-time consultant work. However, if the city does decide
to hire you to do consultant work, you may again become a
public official within the meaning of the Act. See Government
Code Section 82048 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (a)(2)
(enclosed). In this event, Government Code Sections 87100 and
87103(c) would then require you to refrain from participating
in those governmental decisions that would materially affect
your private clients' interests.

Finally, the Act does not prohibit you from doing private
consultant work while you are currently employed by the city
provided, of course, that you refrain from participating in
governmental decisions that might materially affect your
private clients' interests. Please note, however, that there
may be other provisions of law which do prohibit such employ-
ment. Specifically, you may want to check any statements of
incompatible activities adopted for your city under Government
Code Section 1125 et seq.

I hope that this advice is helpful. If you have any
questions, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Dwight E. Dickerson
Legal Counsel
Legal Division

DED:nrw
Enclosure

sections.question: Whether it would be a conflict of interest for your business to participate in the program developed by RCDC, given that you serve on the CETA Private Industry Council for Humboldt County and voted to fund the grant proposal for the program.
sections.conclusion: The Act prohibits you as a public official from participating in governmental decisions, including those of the CETA Council, that could have a foreseeable material effect on your business. The Act does not, however, regulate your private business decisions, including whether or not to participate in the program developed by RCDC.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a CETA Council member who voted to fund a small business program can ethically participate in that program through their own business without violating conflict of interest provisions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: While Government Code Section 87100 prohibits you from using your official position to influence governmental decisions affecting your financial interests, the Act does not restrict your private business decisions to participate in the RCDC program, provided you did not participate in the Council's funding decision affecting your own business.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (dated Feb 29, 1980) and FPPC response (dated March 13, 1980). Additional unrelated letter from Jan 21, 1980 (Dickerson to Pollard) is included in the document. Primary analysis focuses on the Shapiro matter. Minor discrepancy: incoming letter references 'F.I.C.' and 'S.B.S.C.' which appear to be acronyms for CETA Council and RCDC program respectively.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether it would be a conflict of interest for your business to participate in the program developed by RCDC, given that you serve on the CETA Private Industry Council for Humboldt County and voted to fund the grant proposal for the program.

CONCLUSION: The Act prohibits you as a public official from participating in governmental decisions, including those of the CETA Council, that could have a foreseeable material effect on your business. The Act does not, however, regulate your private business decisions, including whether or not to participate in the program developed by RCDC.

Box 837 Sacramento, CA 95804 Dear Dwight, Enclosed is the information on the F.I.C. and the S.B.S.C. as we received it last week. The only additional information which I can think of to add would be that I did vote to fund the grant proposal for the S.B.S.C. embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter to Gary Shapiro regarding whether his ownership interest in Overland Stoneware creates a conflict of interest if the business participates in a small business program that his CETA Council voted to fund. The FPPC concludes that while he cannot participate in governmental decisions affecting his business, he may participate in the program as a private business owner.
